paper_title: PageGuide: Browser extension to assist users in navigating a webpage and locating information

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Users browsing the web daily struggle to quickly locate relevant information in cluttered pages, complete unfamiliar multi-step tasks, and stay focused amid distracting content. State-of-the-art AI assistants (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) and browser agents (e.g., OpenAI Operator, Browser Use) can answer questions and automate actions, yet they return answers without showing where the information comes from on the page, forcing users to manually verify results and blindly trust every automated steps. We present PageGuide, a browser extension that grounds LLM answers directly in the HTML DOM via visual overlays, addressing three core user needs: (a) Find-locating and highlighting relevant evidence in-situ so users can instantly verify answers on the page; (b) Guide-showing step-by-step instructions (e.g. how to change password) one at a time so users can follow and perform actions by themselves; and (c) Hide-hiding distracting content-giving users a chance to decide to hide an element or not. In a user study (N=94), PageGuide outperform unaided browsing across all modes: Hide accuracy improve by 26 percentage points (86.7% relative gain) and task completion time drops by 70%; Guide completion rate increases by 30 percentage points; and Find reduces manual search effort, with Ctrl+F usage falling by 80% and task time decreasing by 19%. Code and demo is at: pageguide.github.io.

sections.title:
1 Introduction
AI chatbots have become a primary tool for information retrieval,
with 62.8% of the internet users citing information retrieval as a top
reason for going online [10]. In WebArena, VisualWebArena, and
WebVoyager [16, 20, 47]) platforms, chatbots read the page content,
autonomously execute actions (e.g., clicking buttons and links or
filling out forms), and return answers in a chat sidebar. Yet despite
this capability, a fundamental problem remains: These autonomous
agents return answers without referencing where they are
extracted from, and act without pausing for user verification.
Users must blindly trust every automated step and manually cross-
check answers against a webpage—making it challenging to inspect,
correct, or intervene (see Figure 1a).
sections.content: Commercial LLM assistants (e.g., RealWebAssist [43], BrowserUse [7], ChatGPT Atlas [30], Dia Browser [36]) and browser-integrated agents (e.g., Claude in Chrome [4], Gemini in Chrome [12], OpenAI Operator [31]) optimize for en-to-end web navigation but make no attempt to ground their outputs in the page or expose intermediate decisions to the user. Grounded reasoning approaches such as HoT [29] and ScreenAI [5] link model outputs to evidence spans, but do so within the models response textthe webpage itself is left unchanged, so users still have to locate the evidence themselves. Standard keyword search (e.g., Ctrl+F) is limited to exact string matching and cannot answer questions or highlight supporting evidence in context. Content filtering tools such as ad blockers [15] rely on fixed CSS rules and cannot adapt to users semantic intent. None of these approaches give users way to verify answers, confirm actions, or semantically hide unwanted contentall on the page. Three specific user needs remain unaddressed. (1) Finding and verifying information on dense, cluttered pages is tedious: users must scan long pages manually to find where AI answers may be generated based on [46]. (2) Completing multi-step tasks with step-level oversight is risky without step-level oversight [8, 19, 21, 24, 27, 37, 41, 44]: users often need to follow many steps (e.g., clicking, scrolling, navigating menus) to complete tasks such as changing password. However, chatbot instructions are long and do not point to where each step should be performed on the page, making them hard to follow. (3) Staying focused amid distracting content is increasingly difficult. While 1 in 3 of the internet users already use ad blockers [15] and prior work shows distraction reduction improves task performance [13, 23], users still lack control over what they want to hide. For example, users may want to temporarily hide political posts during election periods so they can focus on essential tasks and reduce exposure to distracting or emotionally charged content [25, 33]. However, existing tools rely on fixed rules that cannot adapt to users specific goals or to novel webpage layouts [18]. We present PageGuide, browser extension that addresses all three needs by grounding LLM answers directly in the HTML DOM (e.g., specific paragraph, button, or link on the page) via visual overlays. PageGuide offers three main features: (1) Find links model answers to specific HTML DOM elements via inline citations, highlighting supporting evidence in-place so users can instantly verify any claim without leaving the page (Figure 1b); (2) Guide shows step-by-step instructions to perform task, one step at time, with each target element highlighted on the page and the user confirming each step before proceedingfollowing the mixed-initiative copilot model [2, 17, 34, 41] (Figure 2); and (3) Hide interprets natural-language hide request, scores HTML DOM elements against the users intent, and presents reviewable checklist before hiding the elements, each with per-element justification (Figure 3). To evaluate PageGuide, we conduct within-subject controlled user study (𝑁 =94) on websites (Section 3.7), where each participant completed 6 tasks (2 per mode) under both control condition (standard browser tools) and an extension condition, with ordering counterbalanced to mitigate learning effects. We measure task completion time, accuracy, behavioral signals (Ctrl+F usage, page visits), and post-task Likert ratings. Our study aims to answer three central research questions: RQ1: Does PageGuide improve task completion accuracy and success rate across all three features? RQ2: Does PageGuide reduce task completion time and interaction effort (e.g., Ctrl+F usage, mouse clicks, page visits) across Find, Guide, and Hide tasks? RQ3: Do users perceive completing tasks with PageGuide as easier and less effortful compared to completing the same tasks without it? By systematically comparing between two conditions (control and extension), we aim to provide rigorous empirical evidence on whether grounding LLM outputs in the HTML DOM leads to measurable improvements in task efficiency, accuracy, and user experience across all three interaction features. In summary, this paper makes three contributions: (1) unified FindGuideHide framework that grounds LLM answers in the HTML DOM, prioritizing in-situ inspectability over opaque end-to-end automation. (2) controlled user study (𝑁 =94) and benchmark evaluation demonstrating that grounded, verifiable web interaction PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , Figure 2: Given the query How to add ABC to this GitHub project?, PageGuide (powered by Gemini-3-Flash ) generates step-by-step plan and delivers it one step at time. The target UI element is highlighted directly on the page (e.g., Settings , Collaborators )), while the sidebar panel shows the current instruction, the outcome hint ((cid:17)), and Next / Stop controls. The user always drives the pace: each step only advances when Next is explicitly clicked, keeping the user in full control, especially when verification is required (e.g., entering password or confirming the collaborators account). Figure 3: On social platforms such as X.com, users often encounter repetitive or distracting content. Given the query Can you hide advertisements?, PageGuide identifies matching HTML DOM elements and surfaces summary pop-up on the right listing the detected items. The user can review and confirm the selection before the action is appliedeach confirmed element is hidden via CSS display:none , keeping the surrounding layout intact. consistently outperforms both unaided browsing and autonomous agents across all three interaction features. (3) We discuss how HTML DOM-grounded answer design affects perceived ease of use, confidence, and sense of control in AI-assisted browsing, drawing on post-study questionnaire results across all three interaction features.

sections.title:
2 Related Work
Web agents are built to serve a purpose: helping users accomplish
goals on the web more efficiently and accurately than they could
alone. Yet the dominant design paradigm (i.e., fully autonomous
agents that plan, click, and return a final result) conflates com-
pleting a task with serving the user. When a user needs to ver-
ify a claim on a cluttered page, follow a multi-step task, or stay
focused amid irrelevant content, end-to-end automation offers
little help. Prior work can be understood through two orthog-
onal design choices that determine how well a system actually
serves users in these situations: (i) autonomy level, whether the
system acts on behalf of the user or alongside them; and (ii) out-
put grounding, whether the answer is returned without showing
sections.content: where it comes from on the page or linked to specific, visible elements. Systems that score high on autonomy and low on grounding (most existing agents) maximize speed at the cost of verifiability. PageGuide inverts this trade-off: it acts as copilot that grounds every output (e.g., answers, guidance steps, and hiding decisions) directly in the HTML DOM, keeping the user in control at every stage. Web Agents and Browser Automation. LLM-based web agents have made rapid progress on benchmarks that measure end-toend task success [11, 16, 20, 47]. Early systems such as Auto-GPT [32] and BabyAGI [28] decompose high-level goals into sub-tasks, execute them via code or tool calls, and iterate until done. More recently, browser-integrated systemsOpenAI Operator [31], Claude in Chrome [3], Gemini in Chrome [12], Browser Use [7], Dia Browser [36], and ChatGPT Atlas [30]embed LLM planners directly in the browser, enabling click, type, scroll, and navigate actions on pages. All of these systems sit at the high-autonomy, low-grounding end of the design space: they handle the task for the user and return final result, but their intermediate decisions are opaque and they do not show where the output comes from on the page. , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen This opacity carries real costs. Recent benchmarks show that autonomous web agents often make incorrect decisions in high-stakes situationssuch as deleting data, making unintended purchases, or following deceptive Download buttons that expose personal information [8, 21, 24, 37, 44]. partial remedy is the copilot model named Cowpilot [41], where the agent proposes actions and the user is able to pause, reject, or take alternative action when needed. PageGuide extends this idea further by not only requesting confirmation but grounding each proposed action in the specific HTML DOM element it will affect, hinting what will happen next, so users can verify intent before execution rather than trusting an opaque plan. Chain-of-Thought and Grounded Reasoning. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM accuracy by externalizing intermediate reasoning steps [1, 26, 35, 39]. Highlighted Chain-of-Thought (HoT) [29] takes this further by embedding XML-style evidence tags in each reasoning step, linking conclusions to specific input spans so users can trace why an answer is produced. Vision-language work on web interfaces reinforces this direction: ScreenAI [5] and WebQuest [38] show that reliable web interaction requires aligning model outputs with screen elements and multimodal page evidence, not just fluent text. These approaches share common limitation: evidence is surfaced in the models outputa sidebar answer, tagged span, cited regionwhile the webpage in the browser stays exactly as it is. The user sees the model point to evidence but must scroll back to the page and locate it themselves. PageGuide closes this gap by moving evidence directly onto the page: instead of returning the answer is supported by element 42, it places highlight on element 42 in the HTML DOM, making the evidence visible exactly where the user is already looking. This shifts grounding from feature of the models text output into live, interactive layer on the webpage itselfone that users can inspect, override, and act on without switching context. Content Moderation and Selective Filtering. Existing filtering tools operate at the rule level: Ad blockers [15] match URLs and CSS selectors, Purpose-Mode [23] simplifiers hide predefined element classes, and task-focused readers strip non-content regions [13, 15, 23]. These rules are static, require manual updates as page layouts change, and cannot respond to users natural-language intent (e.g., hide posts that feel politically divisive). Research on personalized moderation confirms that users want more agency over what gets filtered and clearer explanations for why [18], yet current tools offer neither: they hide content silently, with no per-element justification and no way for the user to review or override individual decisions. PageGuides Hide mode approaches this problem through the output grounding len: rather than applying fixed rule, it interprets the users intent at query time, scores each HTML DOM element against that intent using an LLM, and surfaces one-sentence hiding reason for every hidden element directly on the page. The user can inspect each decision, restore individual elements, and re-run filtering as new content loads in infinite-scroll feeds. This turns content hiding from an opaque background process into transparent, user-steered interaction. Figure 4: Given user query, the Router assigns it to one of three handlers, after which the agent reads the HTML DOM and produces the corresponding final state. (a) Find (-): for factual lookup queries (e.g., How many episodes in the movie Stranger Things? ), the agent locates supporting evidence spans; the final state highlights relevant elements directly ). (b) Guide (-): for navigation queries on the page (e.g., (e.g., How do change my password? ), the agent iteratively generates actions and incorporates feedback; the final state is the target page reached after completing the steps (e.g., the Change Password form). (c) Hide (-): for content-hiding queries (e.g., Hide political posts ), the agent scores HTML DOM elements based on the users intent; the final state hides the matched elements on the page. Taken together, our work extends these insights by combining HTML DOM-grounded answers, mixed-initiative control, and intent-driven filtering into single browser extension, treating the browser not as surface for automation, but as shared workspace where agent and user can work together.

sections.title:
3 Method
3.1 System Overview
PageGuide is a Manifest v3 Chrome extension that injects content
scripts into webpages, giving it both read and write access to the
HTML DOM in real time. As shown in Figure 4, the system follows
a three-step pipeline: the Router classifies the user’s query and
dispatches it to the appropriate handler; each handler reads the
HTML DOM to ground its answers in the current page state; and
the handler produces a final state by mutating the HTML DOM
directly, highlighting evidence, surfacing step-by-step instructions,
or hiding distracting elements. The three handlers differ in how
they interact with the user after the initial HTML DOM read:
sections.content: Find is single-pass: the agent identifies supporting evidence spans and renders inline highlights on the page in one shot. Guide is iterative: the agent proposes one action at time, the user confirms or skips it (the Feedback loop in Figure 4, and the agent re-reads the HTML DOM on the new page before generating the next step. Hide is single-pass: the agent scores all HTML DOM elements against the users hiding intent and applies PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , display:none to matched elements, attaching one-sentence reason to each. Across all three features, every text output is coupled to HTML DOM mutation: Find returns text answer with clickable inline citations that scroll to and highlight the source element on the page; Guide outputs predicted actions alongside visual highlight on the target element; and Hide hides matched elements in place with an attached reason. The page itself is always the primary output surface. To ensure that these cross-component interactions function correctly in browser (e.g., MS Edge, Chrome), we maintain comprehensive end-to-end testing framework built on Playwright, and integrate it with automated continuous integration (CI) via GitHub Actions. (see Appendix I).

sections.title:
3.3 Intent Router
Once the HTML DOM index D is ready, the Router is the first
component the query reaches Figure 4. Its sole job is to decide which
of the three handlers should run before any page-level interaction
begins. Given query 𝑞 and a compact page context C (e.g., page
title, content type), the router selects:
sections.content: mode = 𝑓router (𝑞, 𝐶), where mode {Find, Guide, Hide}. (1) The router is single LLM call with structured prompt that describes each mode with canonical examples and asks for mode label plus one-sentence justification (see full prompt in Figure 13 in Appendix). Routing by mode allows each pathway to be optimized for its specific task, rather than relying on single monolithic prompt to handle all cases. Router Prompt (condensed) System: Given user query and brief page context, classify the query into one of three features: FIND (factual lookup), GUIDE (step-by-step task), or HIDE (content hiding). Return the mode label and one-sentence justification. User: Query: {query} Page context: {page_title, content_type}

sections.title: Retrieval
sections.content: Find addresses core limitation of existing LLM-based web agents: answers that are fluent but unverifiable because there is no pointer on the screen for users to follow or locate information. Given query 𝑞 and element index D, Find prompts the LLM (see the full prompt in Figure 14 in Appendix) to produce natural language answer in which every factual claim is annotated with an inline citation [N:"exact phrase"] , where is the SoM index of the supporting HTML DOM element and exact phrase is the precise text span to highlight. For example, Find answer for Who directed this movie? might read: The movie was directed by [45:"Nolan"] and produced by [12:"Emma"] . PageGuide then resolves each citation: it locates the HTML DOM node at index N, wraps the matching text span in color-coded animated overlay (colors are drawn from luminance-aware palette so highlights remain distinguishable against any page background), and scrolls the viewport to the first cited element. The final state visible to the user has two coupled components (see Figure 1b): In-page highlights: each cited span is highlighted directly on the page; clicking highlight re-scrolls and re-pulses the element. Side panel answer: the full answer with all citations rendered as clickable anchors is shown in collapsible panel alongside the page. This keeps evidence in situ: the user verifies claim by glancing at the highlighted element on the same page rather than switching context. Find Prompt (condensed) System: Given user query and structured HTML DOM element index, answer the query in natural language. For every factual claim, insert an inline citation in the format [N:exact phrase], where is the element index and exact phrase is the verbatim text span supporting the claim. User: Query: {query} HTML DOM index: {element_id, text, tag, bbox}

sections.title: Navigation
sections.content: Guide targets procedural tasks where users need step-by-step assistance rather than fully automated solution. Given query 𝑞 and element index D, Guide prompts the LLM (see the full prompt in Figure 16 in the Appendix) to produce an ordered action plan 𝑃 = (𝑎1, 𝑎2, . . . , 𝑎𝑘 ), where each step 𝑎𝑖 specifies natural language instruction, the SoM index of the target HTML DOM element, and an action type (click, type, scroll, or navigate). The navigate action handles cross-site tasks by loading new URL and automatically resuming the plan on the updated page. Step-by-Step Delivery. Steps are surfaced one at time. When step 𝑎𝑖 is presented, the target element is highlighted with pulsing beacon on the page, and the instruction appears as tooltip anchored to that element. The side panel displays the instruction and preview of what will happen ((cid:17)), along with two control options (see Figure 2): Next confirms the step, triggers HTML DOM re-read, and advances to 𝑎𝑖+1. Stop ends the session, leaving completed steps in place. For example, the first step for How do change my GitHub password? appears as: Step 1: Click [14:"Settings"] in the top-right corner of your profile. Next Stop , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen (cid:17): You will be taken to account settings where you can update your password. where [14:"Settings"] simultaneously pulses on the page so the user can visually confirm the target before proceeding. The hint previews what the next page state will look like, helping users anticipate the outcome before clicking Next . This confirmation gate is the Feedback loop shown in Figure 4: the user retains full veto power over every action. Guide Prompt (condensed) System: Given user task and structured HTML DOM element index, produce an ordered action plan. Each step must specify: (1) natural language instruction, (2) the target element index, (3) an action type (click, type, scroll, or navigate), and (4) one-sentence hint previewing the expected outcome. User: Task: {query} HTML DOM index: {element_id, text, tag, bbox} Error Recovery. If the page state after confirmed action diverges from the expected outcome (e.g., click produced no navigation), Guide re-reads the current HTML DOM and regenerates the remaining steps from the new page state.

sections.title:
3.6 Hide Mode: Transparent Content Hiding
Hide enables users to hide irrelevant or distracting content using
a natural language description (e.g., “hide all sponsored posts”; see
Figure 3). Unlike static ad-blockers that match fixed CSS selectors
or URL patterns, Hide uses LLM to identify elements that the users
want to hide, adapting to novel page layouts and semantically-
specified criteria.
sections.content: Given query 𝑞 and index D, the LLM (see the full prompt in Figure 15 in the Appendix) returns ranked list 𝐻 of elements to hide, each paired with one-sentence justification and short content snippet. Before any element is hidden, floating summary pop-up is shown to the user. Each row in the dialog displays numbered badge, the hiding reason in purple, the content snippet, and jump button () that scrolls the page to that element so the user can inspect it in context. Every row is checked by default; the user can uncheck any item to exclude it from hiding. Only after the user clicks Confirm does the agent apply display:none to the selected elements. This confirm-before-hide design is deliberate transparency choice: the user reviews and approves every hiding decision before it takes effect, consistent with mixed-initiative principles that preserve meaningful user control [2]. Hide Prompt (condensed) System: Given hiding request and structured HTML DOM element index, identify all elements that match the users intent. For each matched element, return: (1) the element index, (2) one-sentence justification, and (3) short content snippet. Return only elements that clearly match; do not include borderline cases. User: Request: {query} HTML DOM index: {element_id, text, tag, bbox}

sections.title:
3.7 Data Collection
To evaluate how well PageGuide assists users in finding correct
information, guiding them through realistic web tasks, and hiding
specific webpage content, we construct the PageGuide dataset,
sections.content: which consists of three subsets: Find, Guide, and Hide. For details on how each dataset is constructed, please refer to Appendix F. Find Subset. For Find subset, we randomly sample 5 questions from Google Natural Questions [22], which provides questions and answers grounded in webpage content. However, this source alone is not sufficient for the user study, as many examples are centered on Wikipedia-style pages with relatively short content, making the answers easy to find. To better evaluate the efficiency of the Find feature in diverse, long, and content-heavy webpage settings, we augment the task set with 5 additional FIND tasks from other websites. Guide Subset. The goal of the Guide dataset is to examine how well PageGuide assists users in completing web-based tasks through step-by-step guidance. This requires tasks that reflect real user needs and require certain degree of human supervision (refer to Figure 2). Existing datasets, such as Mind2Web [11], OnlineMind2Web [42], and WebArena [47], are primarily designed to evaluate autonomous web agents rather than to examine how an agent assists human user in completing task. As result, some tasks are less appropriate for user study since they are not well aligned with assistive guidance. We therefore construct Guide subset consisting of 138 tasks spanning 37 websites. From this larger task pool, we select 7 representative tasks for the user study. Hide Subset. The objective of the Hide dataset is to study whether PageGuide can hide specific webpage elements in response to users request. This requires tasks that pair hide request with the exact webpage elements that should be concealed. To the best of our knowledge, existing benchmarks do not capture this specific problem. We therefore introduce dedicated Hide subset consisting of 108 tasks, including 67 easy, 22 medium, and 19 hard instances. We define task difficulty based on the number of target item types specified in the user request: easy tasks involve hiding single type of target (e.g., Hide all advertisements on the page), medium tasks involve two types (e.g., Hide all posts that relate to Microsoft or Amazon), and hard tasks involve three or more (e.g., Hide all food items that contain pineapple, pepper and ginger). We then select 10 Hide tasks for the user study. We also evaluate the agents performance on the full Hide dataset to provide broader quantitative context for our user-study results (see Appendix D).

sections.title:
4 User Study
4.1 Study Design
We conducted a controlled user study (N=94) to evaluate whether
PageGuide improves users’ ability to retrieve information, com-
plete procedure tasks an reduce distraction from web-interactions.
Participants complete tasks under two conditions: (1) a control
condition, where they used standard browsing tools (e.g., scrolling,
Ctrl+F) and (2) an extension condition, where PageGuide is enabled.
The study followed a within-subject design with counterbalanced
ordering to mitigate the learning effects.
Participants and Setup. We recruited participants through depart-
mental mailing lists and laboratory networks. All participants were
students, ranging from undergraduate to graduate level, and partic-
ipated voluntarily without compensation. The study (see Figure 11
in Appendix) was conducted in person in a controlled lab setting
sections.content: PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , Figure 5: Task performance comparing control and extension conditions across all three features. Find and Guide are evaluated by accuracy (proportion of correctly completed tasks); Hide is evaluated by accuracy (proportion of target elements correctly identified). PageGuide improves performance in all features: Find (0.81 0.86), Guide (0.23 0.53), and Hide accuracy (0.30 0.56), with the largest gains in Guide and Hide. and lasted approximately 30 minutes per session. Each participant completed six tasks in totaltwo per interaction mode (Find, Guide, Hide)with maximum of three minutes allowed per task. For each mode, one task was completed under the control condition and one under the extension condition. All procedures were reviewed and approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB). For each task, we record the completion time, accuracy, task outcome (completed/partial completed/ failed), and some more finegrained behavioral signals such as number of time user hit Ctrl+F, number of mouse clicks and page navigation patterns. Participants also completed post-task surveys collecting the subjective feedback from the users. The survey questions are designed in Likert-scale and is shown in Figure 9. in helping users more reliably identify all relevant elements compared to manual inspection. For Guide tasks, evaluation is more challenging due to their interactive and open-ended nature. We manually evaluate task correctness by inspecting each participants final screenshot. Only 23% of participants in the control condition successfully reach the correct terminal state, compared to 53% (+30 pp) with PageGuide, as shown in Figure 5. This difference is statistically significant (𝑡 = 5.1, 𝑝 < 108), representing large improvement. This result highlights the difficulty of multi-step procedural tasks without assistance, and demonstrates that step-level guidance of PageGuide with highlighted targets substantially increases users ability to reach the correct outcome.

sections.title:
4.2 Quantitative Results
Next, we present experimental results from our user study and
address the three research questions introduced in Section 1.
sections.content: RQ1) Step-by-Step Guidance Increases Task Completion Accuracy For Find tasks, participant accuracy increases from 81% in the control condition to 86% (+5 pp) with PageGuide as shown in Figure 5. This improvement is consistent with the systems design: by grounding answers directly in highlighted evidence, users are able to verify information in context rather than relying on memory or manual scanning. However, paired Students t-test shows that this improvement is not statistically significant (𝑡 = 1, 𝑝 = 0.32), suggesting modest effect size. This means that while PageGuide provides consistent accuracy gain, the effect size for Find tasks is relatively modest. We attribute this to the already high baseline performance of Find tasks, which leaves limited room for further gains. For Hide tasks, performance is evaluated using accuracy, as the goal is to correctly identify all relevant elements to hide. Accuracy improves substantially from 30% in the control condition to 56% (+26 pp) with PageGuide, as shown in Figure 5. This difference is statistically significant (𝑡 = 4.4, 𝑝 < 105), indicating large performance gain. This result reflects the benefit of PageGuide RQ2a) PageGuide significantly reduces Task Completion Time Across all three tasks, PageGuide leads to faster completion compared to the control condition. For Find tasks, median completion time decreases from 65.2 seconds in the control condition to 52.8 seconds (-12.4s) with PageGuide, as shown in Figure 6. This reduction is statistically significant (𝑡 = 2.31, 𝑝 = 0.024), indicating that grounding answers in highlighted evidence helps users locate information more efficiently. For Hide task, the effect is substantially larger. Median completion time drops from 104 seconds to 31.7 seconds reduction of over 70% as shown in Figure 6. This improvement is highly significant (𝑡 = 9.99, 𝑝 < 1013), reflecting that intent-aware filtering replaces tedious manual inspection. For Guide tasks, we restrict analysis to correctly completed trials to ensure fair comparison, as many control-condition participants prematurely terminated tasks out of frustration. Under this condition, average completion time decreases from 95.8 seconds in control to 66.7 seconds (-29.1s) with PageGuide, as shown in Figure 6. This difference is statistically significant (𝑡 = 4.88, 𝑝 < 104), indicating substantial efficiency gain. These results suggest that, when tasks are successfully completed, PageGuide enables users to complete multi-step workflows more efficiently than , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen Figure 6: Task completion time (seconds) for the control and extension conditions, restricted to correctly completed tasks. Each box shows the median and interquartile range across participants. PageGuide reduces completion time across all three features: Find (65.2s 52.8s), Hide (104s 31.7s), and Guide (95.8s 66.7s), with the largest gain observed in Hide. Figure 7: Behavioral signals (mean SE) comparing the control and extension conditions. Each bar shows the average count or distance per task for five metrics: Ctrl+F presses, text selections, mouse clicks, scroll count, and mouse movement distance. All five metrics decrease substantially with PageGuide, indicating that users rely less on manual search and perform fewer interactions to complete the same tasks. unaided browsing. RQ2b) PageGuide also reduces Manual Search from Users Ctrl+F usage drops from 0.26 to 0.05 (-80%) and text selection frequency decreases from 0.18 to 0.08 (-55%), mouse clicks decrease from 8.22 to 4.78 (-42%), scroll count drops from approximately 13 to 5 (-60%), and mouse movement distance drops from 6968 px to 5490 px (-21%), as shown in Figure 7. Together, these reductions indicate that with PageGuide, users spend less effort scanning, scrolling, and searching for information that is already highlighted in the webpages. Interestingly, page visits remain roughly similar (0.64 vs. 0.68) across conditions. This indicates that PageGuide improves efficiency within each page rather than reducing navigation between pages users still visit similar number of pages, but require fewer interactions to extract relevant information once there. However, these aggregate trends mask important differences across features. Figure 8 breaks down each behavioral signal by mode, revealing that the overall reductions are not uniform. For Find and Hide, reductions are consistent across all signals scroll gestures, Ctrl+F usage, mouse clicks, and mouse distance all decrease, confirming that PageGuide reduces exploratory interaction effort within the page. For Guide, however, page visits (+35%) and mouse movement distance also increases with PageGuide. This is not failure of the system but an expected consequence of its design: Guide actively directs users to navigate across pages to reach the correct destination, so more page visits and cursor movement reflect task-relevant activity rather than aimless browsing.

sections.title:
4.3 Qualitative Results
We collected participants’ subjective feedback through a post-study
questionnaire designed to assess perceived ease of use, accuracy,
and task difficulty across all three interaction features. Ratings
range from 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree) provided in
Figure 12. Using these responses, we address RQ3: do users per-
ceive completing tasks with PageGuide as easier and less effortful
compared to completing the same tasks without it?
RQ3) Users rate PageGuide as easier to use and effective in
helping them locate answers. Participants’ subjective ratings
sections.content: PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , Figure 8: Behavioral metrics (mean SE) broken down by task type (Find, Guide, Hide) and condition. While Find and Hide show consistent reductions across all signals, Guide shows different pattern: page visits and mouse movement distance increase with PageGuide, reflecting that the extension actively guides users to navigate to new pages as part of the task. Figure 9: Post-study Likert ratings (1 = Strongly Disagree, 7 = Strongly Agree) for each interaction mode. Each mode includes three questions: whether PageGuide is accurate or gives correct guidance (F1/G1), whether it makes the task easier (F2/G2/H2), and whether the task would be difficult to complete without it (F3/G3/H3). Bars extending to the right indicate agreement. Find and Hide show the most concentrated positive distributions (8991% agreement on ease of use), while Guide shows slightly more variance, reflecting the added complexity of multi-step procedural tasks. from the post-study questionnaire align with the quantitative results, indicating that users consistently perceived improvements across all three task types. In particular, participants reported that PageGuide made tasks easier to complete, reduced effort, and improved their ability to find, verify, and filter informationmirroring the observed gains in efficiency, accuracy, and accuracy (see Figure 9). For Find tasks, 91% of participants agreed that PageGuide correctly locates needed information (F1), 83% found Find tasks easier with PageGuide (F2), and 51% reported it would be difficult to complete the tasks without it (F3), as shown in Figure 9. The high F1 and F2 scores confirm strong perceived accuracy and usability, while the relatively lower F3 reflects that some Find tasks were simple enough to complete manually. For Guide tasks, 74% agreed that PageGuide provides correct guidance (G1), 77% found tasks easier with PageGuide (G2), and 55% reported difficulty completing tasks without it (G3), as shown in Figure 9. The distributions show more variance compared to Find, consistent with the increased complexity of multi-step procedural tasks. This aligns with the earlier observation that Guide introduces trade-off: while it improves correctness (23% 53%), users may perceive it as slightly more effortful due to the step-bystep interaction. Nevertheless, above 74% positive ratings for both ease and accuracy indicate that users still find the system helpful and usable despite the increased interaction time. For Hide tasks, 89% of participants found tasks easier with PageGuide (H2) and 72% reported difficulty completing them without it (H3) the strongest and most consistent positive ratings across all three features, as shown in Figure 9. This confirms that manual content filtering is perceived as considerably harder, and that PageGuide intent-aware hiding substantially lowers the effort required. , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen Across all three features, ratings shift toward 67 with PageGuide while lower values dominate the without-extension condition (Figure 9). This confirms that users not only perform better but also perceive PageGuide as reducing effort and increasing confidence across all task types. To further understand how perceived experience relates to actual task outcomes, we asked participants to report whether they completed each task by asking the user the question Did you complete the task? with three options (completed / partial / failed) immediately after each trial (Figure 10). Contrasting these self-reported outcomes with the Likert ratings reveals an important nuance. For Hide, the two measures align closely: the large objective gain in completion rate (28% 83%, Figure 10) is matched by the strongest subjective ratings across all features (89% ease of use). This consistency suggests that when task is well-scoped and the agents output is directly verifiable, users both perform better and feel more confident. For Guide, however, subjective ratings (7477% positive) are notably higher than objective completion rates (59%). Examining task outcomes in Figure 10 reveals why: control users tend to either fully complete or fail outright, whereas extension users show higher rate of partial completionsindicating that PageGuide keeps users engaged and prevents early abandonment even when full task success is not reached. This discrepancy suggests that HTML DOM-grounded guidance primarily helps users persist through difficult tasks rather than guaranteeing completion, distinction that motivates future work on adaptive step granularity and multi-turn correction. Figure 10: Task outcome distributions for Guide and Hide tasks, based on participants response to Did you complete the task? after each trial (completed / partial / failed), shown as the percentage of participants per category. With PageGuide, completed outcomes increase and failures decrease in both features. Notably, Guide shows rise in partial completions under the extension condition, indicating that users attempt more steps rather than abandoning the task early. Hide shows the largest overall shift, with most participants reaching full completion.

sections.title:
5 Limitations and Future Work
Single-mode dispatch limits composite tasks. The current in-
tent router maps each query to exactly one mode, which works
well for clearly scoped requests but fails on composite queries that
span multiple features, e.g., “find the settings page and guide me
sections.content: through changing my password requires both Find and Guide step. Future work will replace the single-mode router with multistep planner that decomposes composite queries into an ordered sequence of mode invocations (e.g., Find Guide), handling tasks that currently require users to issue multiple separate queries. Evidence highlights lack cross-page persistence. Finds inpage highlights occasionally overwhelmed participants on dense pages with many matching spans. Additionally, the current implementation highlights only within single page; users who navigate across multiple pages to gather information must re-issue the query each time. Future work will introduce re-ranking step that surfaces the most relevant evidence first, and explore multi-page context: persisting and aggregating highlights across page visits so users can build up cross-page evidence trail within single session. Per-step confirmation in GUIDE may introduce overhead When task time is measured only over correctly completed tasks, PageGuide is consistently faster than the control condition in the GUIDE tasks (Figure 6). The remaining limitation is that the per-step confirmation model adds overhead for users who already know the procedure. Future work will explore adaptive step granularity that compresses self-evident actions into single confirmation, and multi-turn correction (e.g., skip this step, undo the last action) so experienced users can move at their own pace. Content hiding has no memory across sessions. Hides confirmation dialog added review overhead that some participants found cumbersome for long lists of matched elements. More broadly, users currently have no memory across sessions: content they routinely hide must be re-specified each time. Future work will introduce user preference history that persists hiding decisions across sessionslearning what user dislikes over time and applying those preferences automatically on revisited pages, with the ability to review and edit the saved profile.

sections.title:
6 Conclusion
We presented PageGuide, a browser extension that grounds LLM
answers directly in the HTML DOM across three interaction fea-
tures: Find locates and highlights supporting evidence in-place
so users can instantly verify answers; Guide delivers step-by-step
instructions one action at a time with user confirmation at each
step; and Hide hides distracting content with a per-element justifi-
cation shown in a pre-action review dialog. In all three features, the
agent’s output is embedded in the page itself rather than returned
as plain text in a sidebar.
sections.content: A controlled user study (𝑁 =94) against unaided browsing showed consistent gains across all features. Find reduced task time by 15.2% and Ctrl+F usage from 24.1% to 4.4% of tasks, with page visits dropping by 44.3%. Guide improved navigation completion by 7.7% while keeping users in full control of every action. Hide reduced task time by 50.8% and improved content hiding accuracy by 25.7%, enabling users to identify targeted elements they would otherwise overlook. These results support the core thesis of this work: grounded, insitu interactionwhere every answer, step, and hiding decision is anchored to specific HTML DOM elementis more effective than opaque autonomy for tasks where users need to understand why an answer is correct, how procedure should unfold, or what has been PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , changed on page. We hope PageGuide serves as step toward web agents that augment rather than replace human judgmenttools that earn trust by showing their work. Interface Display of the User Study We design unified interface to evaluate PageGuide across different web interaction tasks. As shown in Figures 11a to 11c, each task includes clear instruction panel, timer, and standardized answer interface to ensure consistent evaluation. Prompts This section reproduces the full system prompts used by PageGuides four LLM-backed components. Each prompt is designed for specific role in the pipeline and enforces structured output format that downstream code can parse and act on directly. Questionnaire Display After the experiment, we collected participants overall feedback on their experience using the tools through post-study questionnaire. The questionnaires are designed to capture participants subjective impressions of the system after completing the study tasks. The full questionnaire is shown in Figure 12. Router Prompt (Figure 13): classifies the users natural language query into one of five handlers (find, guide, hide, image_find, pdf_find) and returns JSON object with confidence score and one-sentence justification. , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen Figure 12: Post-Study Questionnaire. Find Prompt (Figure 14): instructs the LLM to answer the users question using [N:"text"] inline citations that reference specific elements from the SoM page index, enabling the frontend to resolve and highlight each cited span. Guide Prompt (Figure 16): produces one step at time as JSON action objectinstruction text, target SoM index, action type, and next-step hint. Hide Prompt (Figure 15): identifies up to 15 page elements matching the users hiding intent, returning each with onesentence justification used to populate the inline placeholder. PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , You are query router for web assistant. Your job is to classify the users query and route it to the appropriate handler. AVAILABLE HANDLERS: 1. "guide" - For step-by-step "how to" questions that need interactive guidance 2. "hide" - For requests to hide, remove, or suppress distracting/annoying content (ads, banners, popups, cookie notices, sidebars, recommendations, etc.) 3. "image_find" - For questions about an UPLOADED IMAGE (finding similar items, comparing with page content) 4. "pdf_find" - For questions about PDF documents (summarize, find specific content, extract info from PDFs) 5. "find" - For questions, information lookup, finding content, highlighting elements (DEFAULT) ROUTING RULES: - "guide": User wants to LEARN how to do something in steps (e.g., "how do report this video?", "where can find settings?", "help me delete my account") - "hide": User wants to hide or remove something on the page (e.g., "hide the ads", "remove the sidebar", "get rid of this popup", "hide recommended videos", "remove the cookie banner", "hide comments", "remove distractions") - "image_find": User asks about their UPLOADED IMAGE - finding it on page, comparing, locating similar items (e.g., "find this product", "where is this item?", "do they have this?", "is my image on this page?", "find similar to my upload") - "pdf_find": User asks about PDF content, document analysis, or mentions PDF explicitly (e.g., "what does this PDF say?", "find in the document", "summarize this PDF", "where does it mention Y?") - "find": Questions about the page, finding information, showing/highlighting elements (e.g., "what is this page about?", "find the price", "show me images", "where is the login button?") sections.title: Intent
sections.content: The intent router is the first and most critical component in PageGuide (in Section 3): misrouted query sends the users request to the wrong execution mode, causing failures regardless of how well Find, Guide, or Hide perform individually. Before evaluating each mode in isolation, we therefore need to establish that the router operates with high reliability across all three task categories. Experiment We constructed labeled evaluation set of 1,208 natural language queries covering three routing classes: find (finding information, 969 queries), guide (step-by-step guidance, 132 queries), and hide (hiding content, 107 queries). Queries for the find class were drawn from the Natural Questions and QASPER datasets [9, 22], guide queries from the Online-Mind2Web benchmark [11], and hide queries from custom dataset collected across 100 diverse websites (see Section 3.7). We report per-class accuracy and error type to identify systematic failure modes. Results The intent router achieves highly reliable 97.68% overall accuracy, confirming that it provides robust gateway for downstream execution  (Table 1)  . It correctly classifies all guide queries (100%) and maintains high accuracy across both the find (97.4%) and hide (97.2%) classes. The 25 errors in the find class are entirely misrouted to guide, misclassification that can arise from ambiguous phrasing (e.g., find me a...) where information retrieval overlaps with navigation intent. Lastly, the hide class errors consist of three queries misclassified as find, which typically occurs when an ambiguous query that do not convey the hide intent clearly (e.g., only want courses that have higher than 4.6 star.). Table 1: Intent router accuracy. Total Correct Accuracy Error Type Class find guide hide 969 132 944 132 104 Overall 1208 1180 97.4% 100.0% 97.2% 97.68% guide 25 find 3 28 total D.2 Gemini-3-Flash is Prefered for the Find task To quantify how accurately Find identifies and highlights supporting evidence, we evaluate it on two question-answering datasets that explicitly require grounding: Natural Questions (NQ) [22], where answers are anchored to specific spans within Wikipedia articles, and QASPER [9], where evidence is grounded within lengthy academic PDFs. Both datasets provide human-annotated evidence spans, making them natural fit for assessing retrieval precision alongside answer quality. Experiment We evaluated Find on two datasets. First, on the NQ development set, we rendered each Wikipedia article as webpage within the extension environment, asking the system to highlight the supporting span(s) and generate an answer to factoid question. We report four metrics for NQ: Precision (fraction of highlighted Table 2: Find accuracy on Natural Questions (NQ). Model Precision Recall F1 Ans. Second, we evaluated Find on the full QASPER dataset (1,189 samples), scientific question-answering benchmark built on NLP papers. Each question is answered using evidence extracted from the corresponding paper. For QASPER, we report Answer F1 (tokenlevel overlap between predicted and gold answers) and Evidence F1 (token-level overlap between predicted and gold evidence spans). and Across both datasets, we compare two backbone models: . For QASPER, we additionally compare against LED-base [6] as , retrieval-specialized baseline. sections.title: Web Tasks
sections.content: To evaluate the performance of Guide, step-by-step nagivator, we use the Online-Mind2Web [11], benchmark of web navigation tasks graded by inherent difficulty. Strong performance here validates that Guide can serve as practical copilot for procedural web tasks, not merely tutorial overlay. , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen Experiment Tasks within the Online-Mind2Web benchmark are stratified into three difficulty splits: Easy, Medium, and Hard. We report the task success rate (%) for each split, comparing two plangeneration backbones: . We additionally include SeeAct [45] as baseline, as it represents two-stage paradigm that decouples action generation from grounding. In contrast, PageGuide adopts one-step grounding approach, directly generating executable actions with grounded references, thereby enabling more tightly coupled reasoning and interaction. The results for SeeAct are taken from the official Online-Mind2Web leaderboard1. and Table 4: Guide task success (%) on Online-Mind2Web by difficulty split. Avg is the mean across splits. Noalso surpasses SeeAct [45] by +5.17 (35.17 tably, PageGuide with vs. 30.00) on average (see Table 4), indicating that tighter coupling between reasoning and grounding in PageGuide may provide advantages over the two-stage design of SeeAct. D.4 Hide Effectively Hides Irrelevant Content We examine the agents ability to selectively hide content based on the user query, preserving only query-relevant information while removing distractions using our collected data as shown in Section 3.7. Experiment We evaluate Hide using Precision (the fraction of hidden elements that are correctly identified as irrelevant), Recall (the fraction of annotated irrelevant elements that are successfully hidden), and F1. Ground-truth annotations label webpage elements as irrelevant with respect to the user query. Both are evaluated. demonstrates superior performance, particularly in Results achieving higher recall, which is key advantage for effective content hiding where under-hiding can frustrate users. As detailed in Table 5, achieves substantially higher recall (59.36 vs. 46.84, +12.52) and F1 (47.42 vs. 41.56, +5.86), indicating it identifies broader set of elements that match the users hiding intent. While ), achieves slightly higher precision (42.82 vs. 45.98, -3.16 for suggesting it applies hiding more conservatively and makes fewer false positive hidings, the overall average shows leading by +7.18 (50.92 vs. 43.74). and Table 5: Hide hiding accuracy on the self-collected dataset (312 samples). Δ Model Details All LLM calls in PageGuide are served through OpenRouter2, unified API gateway that provides access to multiple model providers under single endpoint. We evaluate two Gemini models throughout this paper: google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview google/gemini-3-flash-preview Data Generation Pipeline We construct the dataset through three separate pipelines for Find, Guide and Hide subsets. (refer to Figure 17). Find Subset To construct the Find subset, we collect webpages on the internet and examples from existing resources, such as Google Natural Questions Dataset[22]. We then filter for long, contentdense pages in which the answer is present but not immediately obvious, requiring users to locate specific information within substantial page content. From these pages, we extract requests and manually validate the resulting examples before exporting the final dataset. In total, the Find subset contains 10 questions (see Figure 17A). Guide Subset The construction of Guide subset follows four stage pipeline: collect webpages, select official documentations, extract guide requests and export dataset. (refer to Figure 17b). Firstly, we select popular websites based on global web traffic rankings, using the list of most-visited websites compiled from Similarweb and Semrush [40]. We then collect official documentation, helpcenter articles, and tutorial pages associated with these websites on different common user-facing tasks (e.g., changing setting, applying filter, or navigating to feature.) Next, we extract requests from the help pages and, in some cases, rewrite these procedures into an actual user requests. Finally, we perform manual quality control to remove outdated, ambiguous, or duplicate tasks. The resulting Guide examples each contain natural-language request, the reference length and the corresponding website link. Hide Subset For Hide subset, we first collect diverse set of commonly used webpages across different domains, such as social media, forums, shopping sites, and content platforms. For each page, we save rendered HTML snapshot so that the page structure and visible elements can be inspected during annotation. (refer to Figure 17c) We then build an annotation tool that allows annotators to examine each webpage and hide requests based on the specific content shown on that page. For each request, annotators identify the set 1https://huggingface.co/spaces/osunlp/Online_Mind2Web_Leaderboard 2https://openrouter.ai PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , Figure 17: Data generation pipelines for the three subsets of PageGuide. (A) The Find pipeline collects webpages, filters for long and content-dense pages, extracts find requests, and validates the resulting examples for dataset export. (B) The Hide pipeline collects webpages, filters pages with hideable elements, downloads HTML for offline annotation, manually writes hide requests, marks the target elements to hide, and validates the final dataset. (C) The Guide pipeline collects webpages, selects official documentation, extracts and rewrites guide requests, and validates the resulting examples for dataset export. of page elements that should be hidden and ground the task to the corresponding page content. After annotation, we manually review the tasks to remove trivial, unclear, or duplicate examples and to ensure that each request is feasible and supported by the visible page. The final Hide examples therefore each include webpage, its crawled HTML, hide request, and the grounded target elements to be removed. Beyond Basic Functionality: Enhancing Web Extensions with PDF Reading, Visual Question Answering, and Page-Off Support PageGuide extends beyond the core Find, Hide, and Guide functionalities with three additional features: PDF reading, visual question answering, and Page-Off support. These features are motivated by an important limitation of conventional web extensions: user needs are often not confined to the visible content of the current webpage. In practice, users frequently interact with documents such as PDFs, compare webpage content with uploaded images, or ask questions that go beyond the active tab. Supporting these settings allows PageGuide to provide more complete and flexible browsing experience, rather than restricting assistance to page-bound interactions alone. PDF Reading Many webpages contain or link to information in PDF form, such as papers, reports, manuals, and forms. However, this content is often difficult to browse efficiently using standard web tools. The PDF reading feature allows users to upload PDF and ask questions about its content, enabling the agent to support document-centered information seeking within the same interaction framework as webpage assistance (Figure 18). Visual Question Asking Users also often need to reason about visual content in addition to text. For example, they may want to compare an uploaded object with items shown on webpage, identify whether two images depict the same kind of object, or retrieve page information relevant to visual query. The visual question asking feature allows users to upload an image and ask questions about it. The agent answers by grounding its textual response and visual highlights of the uploaded image in the textual content of the webpage, thereby extending webpage assistance to multimodal interactions (Figure 19). Page-Off Feature Not all user questions are tied to the current webpage. In realistic browsing sessions, users may ask follow-up questions, seek related background information, or shift to new intent while remaining in the extension interface. The Page-Off feature supports such free-form questions that are not directly related to the active page, allowing the extension to remain useful even when the users information need moves beyond the current tab (Figure 20). Limitations of Existing Extensions Existing browser-integrated AI systemsincluding Gemini Chatbot (Figure 21), Browser Use (Figure 22), MolmoWeb (Figure 23), and Gemini Agent (Figure 24)share common limitation: their outputs are not grounded in visible page evidence. These systems return natural language answers in sidebar or chat interface, but they , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. Truong, Runtao Zhou, Trung Bui, Chirag Agarwal, and Anh Totti Nguyen Figure 18: Users can upload PDF and ask questions about its contents through the PDF upload feature. In this example, the user uploads the Attention Is All You Need paper and asks about its main contributions. The agent then provides answers grounded in evidence from the document. Figure 19: PageGuide allows users to upload an image and ask questions about it, while grounding its textual response in evidence from the webpage and specific areas of the users image. In this example, the user asks whether the tiger in the uploaded image is the same species as the tiger described in the webpage, and the agent responds with evidence-backed information. Note: The yellow bounding box highlighting the webpage paragraph is generated automatically by the agent. PageGuide : Browser extension to assist users in navigating webpage and locating information , , do not link claims to specific HTML DOM elements, highlight supporting spans, or provide mechanism for users to verify where the information on the page comes from. In contrast, PageGuide renders evidence highlights directly on the page, making each claim immediately inspectable and easy to cross-reference (Figure 25). viewer integration. Additional edge cases are also tested, including pages with minimal content, extended scrollable layouts, and imagedense documents. Collectively, this framework provides empirical validation that PageGuide operates correctly as fully integrated browser extension, rather than relying solely on the correctness of individual, isolated modules. End-to-End Testing Framework PageGuide employs Playwright as its end-to-end (E2E) testing framework, integrated into an automated continuous integration (CI) pipeline using GitHub Actions. This pipeline is triggered on each push and pull request to the main branch, invoking npm run ci to execute manifest validation, unit tests, and the full Playwright browser suite in sequence. The rationale for E2E testing stems from the inherent limitations of unit testing in the context of browser extension development: numerous failure modes appear only when code executes within real Chromium environment, where it must interact with native browser APIs, extension-specific permissions, and live page contexts. Accordingly, E2E tests are designed to detect integration-level defects that unit tests cannot surface, including extension initialization failures, content-script injection errors, UI regressions, and cross-component interactions among the side panel, options page, and background service worker. The Playwright configuration instantiates Chromium with persistent browser context, enabling the unpacked extension to be loaded via the load-extension and disable-extensions-except launch flags. Test coverage encompasses extension loading and initialization, service worker and background script startup, side panel rendering and user interaction, options page functionality, and PDF Figure 20: With the Page Off feature enabled, users can ask questions that are not related to the currently active tab. The agent answers using information gathered from other webpages on the internet, drawing on its own memory and providing supporting evidence from the internet. , , Nguyen et al., Tin Nguyen, Thang T. In addition, the relevant evidence is embedded within dense text sections, and the highlights include unrelated information, such as male cast members, further reducing the clarity and trustworthiness of the response.
